title: Teeth clipping in newborn piglets
subtitle: When to clip, when to leave, and how to do it without causing harm.
excerpt: Newborn piglets have eight needle teeth (wolf teeth) that can scratch the sow's teats and cause injuries when littermates compete for the udder. Clipping is the routine management decision in larger commercial systems, but it is a painful procedure and welfare codes advise against routine clipping without a clinical reason. Most smallholders with outdoor-reared sows and small litters do not need to clip.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs (2003). AHDB Pork, farrowing and neonatal management guidance, ahdb.org.uk. European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) opinion on tail docking and teeth clipping (2007).

glossaryTerms.definition: The eight deciduous canine and corner incisor teeth present at birth in piglets. Sharp and curved; designed for teat competition. They cause superficial scratches to the sow's udder and facial wounds in competing littermates.

content.text: are a production management issue, not primarily a welfare solution. UK welfare codes require that teeth clipping is only done when there is evidence of injury to the sow's teats or other piglets' faces, not as a routine procedure. For a small litter of 6 to 8 piglets with a well-milked sow, the case for clipping is rarely strong.
Large litters of 10 or more piglets competing intensely for teat positions
Visible teat injuries on the sow causing her to reject or kick off piglets during suckling
Significant facial wounds on piglets' cheeks or noses from teat competition fights

Clip within the first 24 hours of birth; the teeth are softer and the procedure less painful at this stage. Use dedicated side-cutting pig teeth clippers (not standard wire cutters). Hold the piglet firmly with one hand, open the mouth, and clip each of the eight teeth close to but not at the gum line. A clean clip removes the pointed tip; a cut into the gum causes infection and abscesses. Sterilise the clippers between each piglet with a veterinary disinfectant.

After clipping, the tooth should be blunted with no sharp edges and no bleeding at the gum. Check each piglet's mouth for any split tooth or gum injury. A piglet that cries persistently after clipping or refuses to feed should have its mouth inspected for a cut gum, a vet may be needed if infection sets in.

Tooth grinding with a small handheld grinder is a less traumatic alternative to clipping and is used in some welfare-conscious pig units. The technique requires practice; incorrect angle creates heat trauma to the tooth pulp. It is an improvement on clipping if done by someone experienced, but the training requirement is higher.
